Marie Curie was born Maria Salomea Skłodowska on 7 November 1867 in Warsaw, then part of the Russian Empire. She studied physics and mathematics in Paris and earned her degree from the Sorbonne in 1893. After continuing her studies in mathematics, she joined the research group at the University of Paris where she met Pierre Curie.

Marie and Pierre Curie married in 1895 and worked together at the University of Paris. In shared laboratory work they investigated uranium salts and discovered two new elements, polonium and radium. Their research on radioactivity earned them the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1903, shared with Henri Becquerel.

After Pierre Curie died in 1906, Marie Curie took his place as professor at the University of Paris, becoming the first woman to hold a professorship there. She continued her research on radium and its compounds, and in 1911 she received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry for her work isolating pure radium.

During the First World War she helped equip ambulances with portable X-ray units, which became known as petites Curies. After the war she directed the Curie Institute in Paris, where she trained a new generation of researchers. Marie Curie died on 4 July 1934 in Passy, France, from aplastic anemia linked to long exposure to radiation.

Her daughter Irène Joliot-Curie also became a distinguished scientist and later worked at the Curie Institute, continuing the family's research into radioactivity.
